one_line_takeaway: Event-MLLM fuses event-camera streams with RGB via a DINOv2-derived Illumination Indicator and an Illumination-Correction feature-alignment loss to substantially improve MLLM visual reasoning under extreme over/under-exposure, but this is a perception-robustness paper with no adversarial threat model and is off-topic for an AI-security knowledge graph.
key_finding: On the paper's benchmark (Table 1), Ours-7B achieves 53.13% multi-choice accuracy and 85.43% F1, versus the next-best baseline-7B (Qwen-7B without event fusion) at 44.71% accuracy / 81.95% F1 -- an 8.42-point accuracy and 3.48-point F1 gain. For object counting, Ours-7B reaches 74.66% accuracy / 0.4557 MAE versus baseline-7B's 72.73% / 0.5303 and InternVL3-8B's 72.56% accuracy. General MLLMs (LLaVA-7B: 18.43% multi-choice acc; DeepSeek-VL2-Tiny: 7.00%; InternVL3-8B: 31.50%), the event-only model EventGPT (3.16% multi-choice acc), and the illumination-aware Q-Instruct (15.86%) all underperform the event-enhanced models substantially. Ablations (Table 2) show both the Illumination Correction loss alone and the full pipeline (+ LoRA) give incremental gains over the Baseline-3B/7B (e.g., 7B multi-choice accuracy: 44.71 -> 51.90 (Ill-Correction) -> 53.13 (full)). A fusion-strategy ablation (Table 3) shows the paper's two-stage feature-space fusion beats a pixel-level pre-fusion (e.g., 7B multi-choice acc 14.85%) and a simple feature-addition post-fusion (32.24%). Table 4 reports cosine similarity between original and degraded-illumination vision-encoder features: without Illumination Correction this drops from ~0.98 (near-normal brightness) to ~0.65 at extreme brightness ratios (0.05x or 20x); with Illumination Correction it stays above ~0.91-0.93 even at the most extreme ratios, for both Qwen-3B and Qwen-7B backbones.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Event-MLLM, a multimodal large language model that fuses event-camera streams with RGB frames to maintain visual reasoning ability under extreme illumination (severe over- and under-exposure). Its two core technical contributions are: (1) an Illumination Indicator, a learnable signal derived from a frozen DINOv2 branch that quantifies exposure degradation and modulates how event features are fused with degraded RGB features via a set of MLPs; and (2) an Illumination Correction Loss (L_IC), an MSE loss between the fused feature and the feature of the corresponding normal-light image, which trains the fusion module (in a first training stage) to reconstruct normal-light-equivalent semantics from only the degraded RGB and event inputs. A second stage then LoRA fine-tunes the whole MLLM on the fused features for instruction-following. The authors also build the first multi-illumination event-RGB instruction dataset (2,241 triplet samples, 10,129 QA pairs, 17 synthetic brightness rates from 0.05x to 20x, built from four existing event-RGB paired datasets with GPT-4o-generated, human-checked QA) and an accompanying multiple-choice and object-counting benchmark.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a robustness/perception paper about sensor-level illumination degradation (over/under-exposure), not an adversarial or security paper. There is no adversary, attack objective, or threat model in the paper; illumination extremes arise from natural/environmental conditions (e.g., driving scenes), not from a malicious actor manipulating inputs.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation (Section 5) is that Event-MLLM depends on event-camera data being available and would benefit from handling sparse or intermittent event signals; the authors also note their dataset, though the first of its kind, should be expanded to more diverse real-world conditions and instruction types. Additional caveats evident from the text: illumination extremes on RGB frames are synthetically generated at 17 fixed brightness rates rather than being fully naturalistic; the training triplet requires a normal-light reference frame that is not needed (and not available) at inference, so the correction loss is a proxy target rather than a guarantee of correctness; and all comparisons are against the authors' own newly constructed benchmark and dataset, so absolute numbers are not directly comparable to prior published event-VLM or illumination-robustness benchmarks.

datasets_used: Custom multi-illumination event-RGB instruction dataset: 2,241 triplet samples (normal-light frame, extreme-light frame, event frame), ~6 QA pairs each, 10,129 total QA pairs, across 17 synthetic brightness rates (0.05x-20x)

Built from four existing spatially/temporally matched event-RGB paired datasets (DSEC [13], and others cited as [19, 25, 33] including NER-Net+ and an event-assisted low-light video segmentation dataset), with QA generated by GPT-4o and human-checked
Custom instruct-following benchmark with two tasks: Multiple-Choice (scene understanding) and Object-Counting
quantitative_results: Multi-choice accuracy: Ours-7B 53.13% vs Baseline-7B 44.71% vs InternVL3-8B 31.50% vs Q-Instruct 15.86% vs LLaVA-7B 18.43% vs DeepSeek-VL2-Tiny 7.00% vs EventGPT 3.16%
Multi-choice F1: Ours-7B 85.43% vs Baseline-7B 81.95% vs InternVL3-8B 75.46%
Object-counting accuracy / MAE: Ours-7B 74.66% / 0.4557 vs Baseline-7B 72.73% / 0.5303 vs InternVL3-8B 72.56% / 0.8641 vs EventGPT 67.41% / 1.0015
3B vs 7B scaling: multi-choice accuracy 34.48% (Ours-3B) -> 53.13% (Ours-7B); object-counting accuracy 71.08% -> 74.66%
Fusion-strategy ablation (Table 3, 7B): multi-choice accuracy Pre-fusion 14.85%, Post-fusion 32.24%, Ours (two-stage) 53.13%

Feature cosine similarity under extreme underexposure (0.05x) without vs with Illumination Correction: Qwen-3B 0.6597 -> 0.9273; Qwen-7B 0.7420 -> 0.9185. Under extreme overexposure (20x): Qwen-3B 0.6510 -> 0.9138; Qwen-7B 0.6359 -> 0.8939
baselines_compared: Illumination-aware MLLM: Q-Instruct (mPLUG-Owl-2-7B fine-tuned on degraded visual conditions)
Event-Enhanced ablation baselines: Baseline-3B and Baseline-7B (Qwen2-VL-3B/7B without the proposed Illumination Indicator / Illumination Correction Loss)
